title: AI in L&D: A gap for startups and makers - LinkedIn
search_path_label: A 级媒体 / GDELT 路径
full_text:
AI in L&D: A gap for startups and makers
This title was summarized by AI from the post below.
Jim Ralley
8mo
Report this post
The latest AI in L&D report from Egle Vinauskaite and Donald H Taylor tells me one important thing about this industry. There is basically nobody working with actual agents. This is a clear opportunity space for startups and individual tinkerers and makers. I know why. It's because they're really hard to build and hard to implement. But this is where the real potential lies.
10
10 Comments
Like
Comment
Share
Copy
LinkedIn
Facebook
Moses Jaxon
8mo
Report this comment
Curious what u mean by actual agents. I’ve found AI agents aren’t that hard to implement if you start with solid systems first.
That’s the real challenge most L&D teams skip. Without clear workflows and decision logic, an agent has nothing meaningful to automate.
I’ve built lightweight agent systems that replicate the value of 3-5 L&D roles in one — strategist, designer, analyst, copywriter, etc.
I’ve shared my systems approach w leaders amd I’m now helping them spot million-dollar efficiency opportunities. Crazy!
For me, the future of L&D isn’t building fancier prompts — it’s designing systems that let agents work together seamlessly.
#AIinLearning #EnablementSystems #LXD #JaxonLearning #minuteswithmoses
Like
Reply
1 Reaction
To view or add a comment, sign in
More Relevant Posts
Reddy Tirumala Teja
8mo
Report this post
AI looks attractive in concept, but production tells a different story.
High costs and imperfect outputs challenges make it far from a plug & play solution.
For new startups don’t just ride the AI wave on ideas alone.
Run a serious cost and feasibility analysis to be viable.

Dealroom.co — a leading global intelligence platform tracking over 2 million startups — highlights #Pittsburgh in their weekly newsletter. Analyzing startup and innovation economic data, Thomas Macaulay highlights:
• Pittsburgh ranked among the world’s top 20 AI ecosystems, underscoring its growing global influence in this historic economic moment.
• Pittsburgh ranked 10th globally in terms of its AI density, or concentration of research, capital and AI startups relative to its size.
The AI Strike Team is committed to building on this momentum as we unite local, state, and federal leaders behind Pennsylvania’s full-stack advantage.

The AI boom is booming, but is it built to last?
Sequoia Capital partner Alfred Lin warns that most AI startups depend on short-term pilot deals instead of proven, scalable revenue.
Investors are now focusing on measurable growth and long-term business viability, not hype.
Key Points:
• 70% of AI startups rely on non-recurring income.
• Market correction could be coming as reality sets in.
• Encourages founders to focus on sustainable monetization.
Follow MediaBlizz for more insights on AI, tech innovation, and business.

**Building a Robust AI Future: Anthropic's Strategic Move**
Anthropic's decision to bring on Rahul Patil, former CTO of Stripe, highlights the importance of solid infrastructure in AI development. As we witness the rapid evolution of artificial intelligence, ensuring that foundational systems can support innovation has never been more crucial.
At ElevenX Capital, we believe that strategic hires like these are essential for startups to scale effectively. How do you assess the impact of leadership changes on a tech company's future?

AI Isn’t Replacing You — It’s Absorbing You.
This week’s Tribe Weekly isn’t about fear-mongering. It’s about strategy.
Founders, the real risk of AI isn’t job loss. It’s power loss — slowly getting absorbed into platforms you don’t control.
This issue breaks down:
🧠 What foundational models really mean for startups
💥 How to build smarter, not bigger
📍 The launch of Startup Huddle Halifax — a new space for real talk, real feedback, and founder growth
🎉 Tribe’s nomination at the Tech Forward Awards
➡️ Read the full issue and find your edge in this fast-moving AI era.

8mo
Report this post
Founders seeking funding see U.S. investors as better fit for sector’s fast pace and huge upfront costs
Quick Summary
European AI founders are increasingly seeking funding in the U.S. due to deeper capital pools and a more risk-tolerant culture.
U.S. AI and machine-learning startups raised over $160 billion this year, significantly more than Europe’s approximately $20 billion.
American investors accounted for 71.1% of European AI VC deals by value this year, up from 57.5% last year.

Startup Boston

8mo
Report this post
AI Gold Rush or Bubble? 💰 Or maybe… both?
Every founder feels it - the pressure to move faster, integrate smarter, and not get left behind. But under the glitter and buzz, what’s really happening?
At Startup Boston Week 2025, we asked investors and operators who’ve survived every tech boom and bust to share what’s hype, what’s real and who’s actually cashing in.
👀 Spoiler: the answers might surprise you.
We learned from:
- Maia Heymann ( Converge VC )
- Chip Hazard ( Flybridge )
- Jonathan Corbin ( Maven AGI )
- Heena Purohit ( Microsoft for Startups )
- Habib Haddad ( E14 Fund )
5 takeaways you can use now:
1️⃣ AI is everywhere—execution wins. Not every “AI startup” will be a winner
2️⃣ Back the team, not the spreadsheet. Early-stage = people, grit, and customer obsession
3️⃣ Moats move. Speed to learn/ship > temporary tech edges
4️⃣ Adopt fast, keep trust. Responsible rollout and uncertainty detection matter
5️⃣ Next wave: physical AI, robotics, and richer human–computer interaction
Blog with key insights, transcript & video link: 👇
📖 https://lnkd.in/e8Ez9Rb3
#BostonStartups #AI #VentureCapital #Founders #SBW2026 #GoToMarket #ProductStrategy
19
Like
Comment
Share
Copy
LinkedIn
Facebook
To view or add a comment, sign in
Tara Dowdell
7mo
Report this post
TechCrunch Disrupt. Ironically, the most “human” insight I took from the AI-heavy exhibition was that innovation still depends on people - curious, creative, risk-taking people. AI can assist, but it doesn’t replace vision. AI is moving fast, investment follows momentum - I get it. But not every breakthrough will come from code; some will still come from chemistry, physics, and materials. We still need people building things you can touch, not just train. Let's not lose sight of those hard tech entrepreneurs working to create the tangible foundations that make AI (and everything else) possible.
#Innovation #Startups #FutureOfWork #Deeptech #Hardtech #MaterialsScience #entrepreneurs #founders
14

Report this post
“It’s 1996 for AI.”
That’s how one speaker put it at DC Startup & Tech Week (Formerly DC Startup Week) — and they’re right.
In my latest blog, I break down what that means for startups and why now is the time to engage with Washington.
Link in comments 👇
#DCSTW25

Let's not lose sight of those hard tech entrepreneurs working to create the tangible foundations that make AI (and everything else) possible.

AI in L&D: A gap for startups and makers
This title was summarized by AI from the post below.
Jim Ralley
8mo
Report this post
The latest AI in L&D report from Egle Vinauskaite and Donald H Taylor tells me one important thing about this industry. There is basically nobody working with actual agents. This is a clear opportunity space for startups and individual tinkerers and makers. I know why. It's because they're really hard to build and hard to implement. But this is where the real potential lies.

Moses Jaxon
8mo
Report this comment
Curious what u mean by actual agents. I’ve found AI agents aren’t that hard to implement if you start with solid systems first.
That’s the real challenge most L&D teams skip. Without clear workflows and decision logic, an agent has nothing meaningful to automate.
I’ve built lightweight agent systems that replicate the value of 3-5 L&D roles in one — strategist, designer, analyst, copywriter, etc.
I’ve shared my systems approach w leaders amd I’m now helping them spot million-dollar efficiency opportunities. Crazy!
For me, the future of L&D isn’t building fancier prompts — it’s designing systems that let agents work together seamlessly.

AI Gold Rush or Bubble? 💰 Or maybe… both?
Every founder feels it - the pressure to move faster, integrate smarter, and not get left behind. But under the glitter and buzz, what’s really happening?
At Startup Boston Week 2025, we asked investors and operators who’ve survived every tech boom and bust to share what’s hype, what’s real and who’s actually cashing in.
👀 Spoiler: the answers might surprise you.
We learned from:
- Maia Heymann ( Converge VC )
- Chip Hazard ( Flybridge )
- Jonathan Corbin ( Maven AGI )
- Heena Purohit ( Microsoft for Startups )
- Habib Haddad ( E14 Fund )
5 takeaways you can use now:
1️⃣ AI is everywhere—execution wins. Not every “AI startup” will be a winner
2️⃣ Back the team, not the spreadsheet. Early-stage = people, grit, and customer obsession
3️⃣ Moats move. Speed to learn/ship > temporary tech edges
4️⃣ Adopt fast, keep trust. Responsible rollout and uncertainty detection matter
5️⃣ Next wave: physical AI, robotics, and richer human–computer interaction
Blog with key insights, transcript & video link: 👇
